sect1.title: 29.1 Overview and Heritage
sect1.para:
The Island of Puerto Rico is home to a population of approximately 3.5 million persons. The Puerto Rican community, comprising over 5.5 million individuals, represents the second largest demographic group in the continental United States accounting for nearly 10% of the Hispanic communities. They self-identify as Puertorri-queños or Boricua (the Taíno Indian word for Puerto Rican) or Niuyoricans for those born in New York. Diversity among Puerto Ricans includes assimilation, individuality, and the variant characteristics of culture as presented in Chap.
1
.

Puerto Rico is a commonwealth of the US Citizenship status, which has created a controversial va y ven (go and come) circular migration in which individuals and families are often caught in a reverse cycle of immigration, alternately living a few months or years in the United States and then returning to Puerto Rico. Some Puerto Ricans fear that the dominant American culture is a potential threat to the Puerto Rican culture, language, and future.

Although the educational system in Puerto Rico is similar to that in the United States, when children emigrate from Puerto Rico to the mainland United States, many educational organizations place them one grade below their previous academic year, sometimes due to language barriers.

listitem.para: English and Spanish are both official languages.

Most Puerto Ricans speak with a melodic, high-pitched, fast rhythm maintained when speaking English. Avoid making comments about accent, use caution when interpreting voice pitch, and seek clarification when in doubt about the content and nature of a conversation that may seem confrontational.

Many prefer to read or share sensitive information, options, and decisions with close family members. Some obtain assistance from extended family or community members knowledgeable in health matters. Seek clarification of the information provided, ask for language preference in verbal and written communication, and allow time to exchange information with questions and answers when critical decisions need to be made.
Great value is placed on interpersonal interactions such as simpatía when an individual is perceived as likable, attractive, and fun-loving.

Most individuals enjoy sharing information about their families, heritage, thoughts, and feelings and expect the healthcare provider to exchange personal information when beginning a professional relationship.
If confianza (trust) is established, healthcare providers can establish open communication channels with individuals and families.

Most Puerto Ricans readily express their physical ailments and discomforts, except for sexuality, which is often considered taboo. Set boundaries with discretion, emphasizing personal rather than impersonal and bureaucratic relationships.

Among younger generations and those born in the United States, eye contact is maintained and is often encouraged, with the more traditional, limited eye contact being a sign of respect, especially toward older people.

The culture of Puerto Rico places significant importance on the values of reverence and nurturing toward the elderly population. Clinicians need to acknowledge and attend to the concerns and preferences of elderly patients while recognizing the significance of involving family members in the decision-making process and incorporating them into healthcare discussions when suitable.
Hand, leg, head, and body gestures are commonly used to augment messages expressed by words. Feelings and emotions are also expressed through touch.
Gesturing includes an affirmative nod with an “Aha!” response which does not necessarily mean agreement or understanding related to the conversation.

Women greet each other with a robust and familiar hug, and if among family or close friends, a kiss is included. Young women may take offense to verbal and nonverbal communications that portray women as non-assertive and passive. Men may greet other men with a firm handshake and a left hand stroking the greeter’s shoulder.

para.emphasis: Greet Puerto Ricans formally or with a friendly handshake.

listitem.para: Most individuals are present-oriented, having a relativistic and serene view and way of life.

para.emphasis: Carefully explain appointment times and time limits at the beginning of an interview.

listitem.para: Respect is reflected in how children talk, look, and refer to adults and older people. Rather than Señora (Mrs.) and Señor (Mr.), children and adults are expected to use the terms Doña (Mrs.) and Don (Mr.) for most adults. Aunts and uncles have their names preceded by tití or tío (auntie/uncle) and madrina or padrino (godmother or godfather).

A single woman may use her name as Sonia López Mendoza, with López being her father’s surname and Mendoza her mother’s. When she is married, the husband’s last name, Pérez, is added with the word de to reflect that she is married. This woman’s married name would be Sonia López de Pérez; the mother’s surname is eliminated. In business and health-care organizations, Señora López de Pérez is the correct formal title for promoting conversation or building relationships. Ask patients their complete name as well as their legal name.

Some parents, hailing from conservative backgrounds, believe that children do not have to engage in conversations about sex with them. Some women perceive parents lacked awareness of children’s sexual activity during adolescence or before marriage.

Sometimes, parents may struggle to initiate open and candid conversations regarding sexual matters with their children due to cultural norms that deem discussions about sex taboo within the household. Information about sex is predominantly facilitated through the educational system, with teachers and peers playing a crucial role in the education of young individuals. Involving Puerto Rican parents in conversations regarding sexual health education can pose specific difficulties attributed to potential unease surrounding the subject matter. Establish trust and cultivate an environment conducive to open communication. Offer guidance on engaging in conversations with children about safe sexual practices. Educate against sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and unintended pregnancies while underscoring the importance of privacy and consent. Ensure that their children’s health information is kept confidential. When language becomes a barrier, it is advisable to use interpreters to facilitate clear communication.
Traditional families may view women as lenient, submissive, and wanting to please men. Accept family decision-making styles without judgment.

Traditional cultural norms discourage an overt sexual-being image for women, but many of these conventional values disappear with family assimilation. Topics such as sex, sexually transmitted infections, or other infections establish an environment built on
confianza
and
personalismo
. Employ culturally sensitive terminology and refrain from presumptions or imposing your views. Promote an environment conducive to open communication where clients are encouraged to articulate their opinions and share their concerns. Refer patients to specialty care or community support groups with specialized expertise in working within the Puerto Rican cultural context.

Most families expect their children to stay home until they get married or pursue a college education. The mother is expected to actively discipline, guide, and advise children. Most fathers expect to be consulted but mainly see themselves as financial providers. Address family conflict within the context of the family to promote adolescents’ mental health issues rather than using individual approaches.
Many traditional families socialize male children to be robust.
Female children are socialized with a focus on home economics, family dynamics, and motherhood.

Many Puerto Rican mothers use threats of punishment, guilt, and discipline, which can create stress and difficulties for adolescents as they struggle with the more permissive cultural patterns of the United States such as dating. Provide counseling and explain US child abuse laws.
The family structure may be nuclear or extended. Family members include grandparents, great-grandparents, married children, aunts, uncles, cousins, and even divorced families with their children.

Dependent older people are expected to live with their children and be cared for. Placements in nursing homes and extended-care facilities may be considered inconsiderate and cause family members to feel guilty and experience depression and distress. Explore alternatives for long-term care and provide information to all family members involved in this decision-making process—address discharge planning and hospice and palliative care. Develop strategies for providing emotional support and assistance to family members caring for older adults.
A family member is expected to be at the sick person’s bedside. Ask for the name of the family spokesperson and document it in the patient’s chart.

Some families may have difficulty educating young women about sexuality and reproductive health. Thus, adolescents depend on educational organizations to learn about sexual health, including body changes such as menstruation and the reproductive system. Educating the family about sexuality issues gains respect and entrance into the trusted family environment.

Homosexuality is predominantly a taboo topic that carries a tremendous cultural stigma among most Puerto Ricans. Some men may perceive sexual intercourse with men as a sign of virility and sexual power rather than homosexual behavior. Do not disclose same-sex relationships without the patient’s consent. When providing care for individuals who identify as lesbian, gay, bisexual, or transgender, it is vital to inquire about their sexual orientation status, specifically whether they have openly shared this information (disclosed) or have chosen to keep it private (undisclosed).

Some have a relativistic view of time and may not value regular attendance and punctuality in the workforce. Explain the timeliness requirement in the dominant American (and other countries) workforce and any repercussions for chronic tardiness.

Most Puerto Ricans value personal relationships at work. Work is perceived as a place for social and cultural interactions, including listening to background music while performing job activities. This practice may lead to loud, cheerful, and noisy conversations requiring the employer’s attention. Explain the culture of the dominant American workforce etiquette and the allowable noise level.
For many women, family responsibilities, pregnancy, and the health of their children and other family members are prioritized over work. Negotiate flexible work hours when possible.

Although most Puerto Ricans are bilingual, some speak broken or street English, such as Spanglish . Communicate composed and articulately, employing straightforward vocabulary and expressions that can aid in promoting comprehension.

Among younger generations and those born in the mainland United States, eye contact is maintained and is often encouraged. With the more traditional, limited eye contact is a sign of respect, especially with older people. Ask questions and do not assume that the lack of eye contact means the person is not listening, caring, or being less than truthful.

The Puerto Rican population displays diverse skin tones, encompassing individuals with fair, olive, and darker complexions. The diversity observed can be attributed to the prevailing ancestral influences characterized by European, Spanish, African, and Native American physical traits.
The facial features of individuals of Puerto Rican descent exhibit considerable diversity due to their varied ancestral backgrounds.

Table 29.1 presents commonly occurring health conditions and diseases for Puerto Ricans in three categories: lifestyle, environment, and genetics. See Chap.
2
.

para.emphasis: Advise patients and families traveling to Puerto Rico to avoid exposure to endemic areas and always to use mosquito repellent and protective clothing. Become familiar with dengue fever’s signs, symptoms, and treatment recommendations.

table.title: Table 29.1 Health conditions common to Puerto Ricans

listitem.para: Puerto Ricans have shown high rates of alcoholism. Use validated screening tools such as the brief version of the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT), to assess individuals for alcohol misuse. Recommend patients to specialized treatment programs, such as outpatient counseling, inpatient rehabilitation, or mutual support groups. Conduct evaluations on patients to ascertain the coexistence of comorbid mental health disorders that commonly co-occur with alcoholism such as depression or anxiety. Develop a regular schedule of consecutive sessions to methodically assess the progress of the individual, address any potential relapses, and make adjustments to treatment.

Puerto Rican men and women have a high smoking prevalence. Assess smoking habits and promote programs aimed at helping individuals to quit smoking. It is beneficial for young children and adolescents to establish networks with schools, beginning in elementary school, to support healthy behaviors.

Lack of condom use may be a significant risk behavior. Some men fear that if they use condoms, they portray a less macho image, have decreased sexual satisfaction, or portray that they have a sexually transmitted infection or HIV. Some women may refuse a condom, believing the man thinks she is “dirty.” Provide choices such as contraceptive methods or pharmaceutical interventions.

Puerto Ricans celebrate, mourn, and socialize around food. Food is used (a) to honor and recognize visitors, friends, family members, and health-care providers, (b) as an escape from everyday pressures, problems, and challenges, and (c) to prevent and treat illnesses. Patients may bring homemade goods to healthcare providers to express appreciation, respect, and gratitude for services rendered. Refusing food offerings may be interpreted as a personal rejection.
Being overweight is a sign of health and wealth. Some individuals eat to excess, believing that if they eat more, their health will be better. Educate and negotiate a healthy weight with patients.

Diets include roots called viandas, vitamin-rich vegetables, and starches. The most common are celery roots, sweet potatoes, dasheens, yams, breadfruit, breadnuts, green and ripe plantains, green bananas, tanniers, cassava, and chayote squash or christophines. The dietary habits of Puerto Ricans often consist of processed foods while lacking in fresh fruits, vegetables, and whole foods. This dietary pattern may contribute to the development of many health disorders, including obesity, heart disease, and diabetes. In turn, it is crucial to evaluate Puerto Ricans’ access to nutritious food options and consider providing referrals to social workers to address this issue. Familiarize with some traditional foods and their nutritional content to assist families with dietary practices that integrate their conventional or preferred food selections. Table 29.2 lists a variety of typical Puerto Rican food and ingredients.

Many families believe a healthy child is gordita or llenito (diminutive for fat or overweight) and has red cheeks. Educate mothers and other family members about healthy nutrition and the health risks for children who are overweight.

A traditional breakfast includes hot cereal such as oatmeal, cornmeal, or rice and wheat cereal cooked with vanilla, cinnamon, sugar, salt, and milk. Although less common, many eat corn pancakes or fritters for breakfast. Lunch is served by noon, followed by dinner at around 5 or 6 p.m.

Rice and stew habichuelas (beans) are the main dishes. Rice may be served plain or cooked with many starch-rich side dishes. Rice cooked with vegetables or meat is considered a complete meal. Arroz guisado (rice stew) is seasoned with sofrito, a blend of spices such as cilantro, recao (a type of cilantro), onions, green peppers, and other nonspicy ingredients. Rice is cooked with chicken, pork, sausages, codfish, calamari, or shrimp. It is also cooked with corn, several beans, and gandules (green pigeon peas), a Puerto Rican bean rich in iron and protein.
Various pastas, breads, crackers, vegetables, and fruits are eaten. Fritters are also common foods. Fried green or ripe plantains are a favorite side dish.

Many ascribe to the hot–cold classifications of foods for nutritional balance and dietary practices during menstruation, pregnancy, the postpartum period, infant feeding, lactation, and aging. Table 29.3 identifies many of the foods that are considered either “hot” or “cold.” Becoming familiar with dietary practices facilitates the provision of expert recommendations for developing culturally sensitive food alternatives.

Understanding that iron is considered a “hot” food not usually taken during pregnancy can help healthcare providers negotiate approval. Educate women about the importance of adhering to iron recommendations, even during pregnancy and lactation. Demonstrate recognition and reverence toward these beliefs while delivering evidence-based knowledge regarding the significance of sufficient iron consumption and viable avenues for obtaining iron-rich dietary options. Participating in inclusive and culturally aware dialogues can facilitate the reconciliation of disparities between cultural values and medical recommendations.
A summary of food habits, reasons for practices, and recommendations for health-care providers during such developmental stages is included in Table 29.4 .

Black cohosh, evening primrose, St. John’s wort, ginkgo, ginseng, valerian root, sarsaparilla, chamomile, red clover, linden leaves, and passionflower are the most common herbs. Discuss the safety and efficacy of the most frequently used alternatives. Include the use of complementary and alternative therapies in routine health assessments.
If the individual is believed to have low blood pressure and is weak or tired, brandy may be added to black coffee to enhance the work of “an old heart.”

para.emphasis: Exercise sensitivity toward folk practices as they can discourage patients from seeking further medical care and undermine their trust and confidence. This inquiry pertains to nutritional practices, focusing on integrating culturally sensitive meal choices or non-conflicting behaviors into dietary regimens.

listitem.para: During illness, chicken soups and caldos (broth) are used as a hot meal to provide essential nutrients.

A honey, lemon, and rum mixture is used as an expectorant and antitussive. A malt drink, malta (grape juice), or milk is often added to an egg yolk mixed with sugar to increase the hemoglobin level. Ulcers, acid indigestion, and stomach discomfort are treated with warm milk, with or without sugar.

Herbal teas are used to treat illnesses and to promote health. Most herbal teas do not interfere with medical prescriptions. Incorporating herbal tea with traditional Western medicine may enhance treatment compliance.

table.title: Table 29.2 Common Puerto Rican meals and fritters

entry.para: Green plantain fritters filled with meat or crab

para.emphasis: Pastelillos de carne, queso o pasta de guayaba

entry.para: Turnovers filled with meat, cheese, or guava paste
Traditional dish mainly served during the Christmas season

table.title: Table 29.3 Puerto Rican hot–cold classification of health-illness status, medications, herbs, and selected foods

entry.para: G. I. illnesses (constipation, diarrhea, Crohn’s colitis, ulcer bleeding)
Osteomuscular illnesses (arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis)

table.title: Table 29.4 Puerto Rican cultural nutrition and health beliefs and practices during health stages

entry.para: Food taboos: Avoid spices, cold beverages, acidic citric fruits and substances, chocolate, and coffee
It may induce cramps, hemorrhage clots, and physical imbalance. It may produce acne during menstruation
Assess individual beliefs and acknowledge them. Incorporate traditional beliefs with treatments as required in nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories for dysmenorrhea
Foods encouraged: Plenty of hot fluids such as cinnamon tea and milk with cinnamon and sugar. Teas such as chamomile, anise seed, linden tea, mint leaves
Fluids encourage body cleaning of impurities. Hot beverages promote circulation and reduce abdominal colic, cramps, and pain. Teas are soothing to all body systems
Encourage passive exercise. Provide information about the role of exercise in the reduction of menstrual pain. Support other practices
Health practices: Avoid exercise and practice good hygiene. Do not walk barefoot. Avoid wind and rain. Stay as warm as possible

Exercise may increase pain and bleeding. Good hygiene is important for health. Walking barefoot during menstruation may cause rheumatoid arthritis and other inflammatory diseases. Warm temperatures promote circulation and the health of the reproductive system, as well as prevent cramps
Food taboos: Hot foods, sauces, condiments, chocolate products, coffee, beans, pork, fritters, oily foods, and citric products
It may cause excess flatus, acid indigestion, bulging, and constipation. Chocolate and coffee may cause darker skin in the fetus. Some believe citric products may be abortive
Encourage healthy food habits. Provide information about chocolate and coffee myths. Encourage fruits
Discourage the use of raw eggs in beverages because of the possibility of salmonella poisoning
Food encouraged: Milk, beef, chicken, vegetables, fruits, and ponches
Considered healthy and nutritious. Increase hemoglobin and strengthen and promote good labor
Encourage the use of food recommended for pregnancy. Provide information about sexual activity
Health practices: Rest and get plenty of sleep. Eat plenty of food. Follow diet cautiously. Many avoid sexual intercourse early in pregnancy. Practice good hygiene and take warm showers
It enhances health and prevents problems during birth. Sex may cause issues with the baby or preterm labor
Encourage a balanced plan of exercise with emphasis on weight control and the health of the baby
Food taboos: Avoid beans, cabbages, lettuce, seeds, nuts, pork, chocolate, coffee, and hot food items at all times
These foods cause stomach illnesses for the infant and mother, including baby colic, diarrhea, and flatus
Include a dietary plan that is balanced with substitute food items. Clarify any myths about infant diarrhea, colic, and flatus
Food encouraged: Milk, water, ponches, chicken soup, chicken, beef, pastas, and hot cereals
Improve health and increase hemoglobin and essential vitamins. Protect mother and infant from illnesses. Fluids and ponches increase milk supply. Red meats reduce cravings
As above with raw eggs. Provide information about reasons for stroke and facial paralysis. Provide time to ask questions and reduce anxiety during winter season deliveries
Health practices: Avoid cold temperatures and wind. A few may avoid showering for several days after birth. Great attention is paid to health of the mother
Cold temperatures and winds are believed to cause stroke and facial paralysis in a new mother. Showering may cause respiratory diseases. Mother is believed to be at risk and fragile
Food taboos: Beans, too much rice, and uncooked vegetables
It is believed to cause stomach colic, flatus, and distended abdomen. Too much rice causes constipation
Provide information about appropriate dietary patterns for infant

Foods encouraged: Hot cereals, ponches, chicken broth, or caldos. Fresh fruits, cooked vegetables, viandas (raw eggs, cereals, baby food in milk bottles). Fresh fruit juices. Mint, chamomile, and anise tea. Sugar and honey used for hiccups

Believed to be nutritious, healthy, and to decrease hunger. Caldos are fortifying and preventing illness. Cooked vegetables are healthy and avoid constipation. Bottle food fills the baby. Fresh juices and fruits refresh the stomach. Teas help babies sleep and cure flatus. Sugar and honey have curing properties

Instruct about infant diet and timely introduction of food items to diet. Explain the consequences of excessive weight in infants. Discourage food in a bottle to prevent choking. Discourage raw eggs because of the risk of salmonella and egg allergies and the use of honey because of the risk of botulism. Teas are harmless and provide additional fluid when used in moderation without sugar
Health practices: Keep baby warm while feeding
Warm babies eat, chew, and digest food better, and choking is decreased
Provide information about choking and babies
Health practices: Some persons may believe they can alleviate discomfort by relying on faith and engaging in prayer
Praying or invoking the Christian God to seek relief from physical discomfort or illness

Evaluate dietary practices, including habits. Encourage prayer if it brings comfort and peace, emphasize medical treatment’s significance, and respect individuals’ beliefs. Puerto Ricans’ beliefs around worship have the potential to offer emotional and psychological benefits, which can have an indirect positive impact on their overall Well-being

listitem.para: Due to the Catholic Church’s endorsement of “natural” family planning methods and sexual abstinence, women often refrain from using contraceptives such as foams, creams, and diaphragms, as they are regarded as morally objectionable. Historically, abortion has been predominantly seen as unacceptable, with limited exceptions granted when the mother’s life is at risk. However, it is worth noting that this perspective is shifting, with an increasing number of women embracing this option.
Many women tend to initiate prenatal treatment later in their pregnancies. Encourage early prenatal visits and explain their importance in having a healthy pregnancy and newborn.

Men are socialized to be tolerant, understanding, and patient regarding pregnant women and their preferences. Women are encouraged to rest, consume large quantities of food, and carefully watch what they eat. Some individuals expect women to “get fat” and place little emphasis on weight control. Strenuous physical activity and exercise are discouraged, and lifting heavy objects is prohibited.
Many women refrain from tener relaciones (having sexual intercourse) after the first trimester to avoid hurting the fetus or causing preterm labor.

Loud and verbally expressive behaviors are culturally accepted and encouraged to cope with pain and discomfort. Pain medications are welcomed. Most women oppose having a cesarean section because it portrays a “weak woman.” Discuss the possibility of a cesarean section early in the pregnancy. Explain reasons for the necessity of invasive interventions during labor. Encourage using social support networks comprised of mothers who have undergone comparable experiences.

Women are encouraged to avoid exposure to wind and night lower temperatures, not to lift heavy objects, and not to do housework for 40 days after delivery. Some traditional women do not wash their hair during this time.

Mothers are encouraged to drink fluids such as milk and chicken soup if breastfeeding. They drink milk or fresh juices mixed with raw egg yolk and sugar if they feel weak or tired. Hot foods such as chocolate, beans, lentils, and coffee are discouraged because they are believed to cause stomach irritability, flatus, and colic for the mother and infant.

Some women believe that breastfeeding increases their weight, disfigures their breasts, and makes them less sexually attractive and undervalue the benefits of breastfeeding. Puerto Rican women exhibit inferior evidence in several health habits and outcomes, such as lower exclusive breastfeeding rates.

para.emphasis: Provide information about breastfeeding practices and educate women about myths and misconceptions. The inclusion of grandmothers and significant others in breastfeeding programs is encouraged due to their impact on breastfeeding practices.

listitem.para: Death is perceived as a time of crisis. The body is considered sacred and is guarded with great respect.

Give news about the deceased to the head of the family first, usually the oldest daughter or son. Use a private room to communicate such notice and have clergy or ministers present when the news is disclosed. Allow time for the family to view, touch, and stay with the body before it is removed.
Burial rituals may be delayed until all close family members can be present. Cremation is rarely practiced.
Among Catholics, religious ceremonies such as praying, velorio (wake), novenas, and 9 days of saying the rosary follow the death.

Families freely express themselves through loud crying and verbal expressions of grief. Some may talk in a thunderous way to God. Others may express their sorrow through sensitive but continuous crying or sobbing. Some believe that not expressing their feelings could mean a lack of love and respect for the deceased. Some develop psychosomatic symptoms; others may experience nausea, vomiting, or fainting spells due to an ataque de nervios (nervous attack). Be nonjudgmental with patients’ expressions of grief. Provide a private environment to help minimize interruptions.

Most Puerto Ricans are Catholic; however, many have joined Evangelical churches because they offer a more personal spiritual approach. Many individuals who adhere to religious beliefs often rely on the concept of a higher power and miracles to overcome challenging circumstances.

A few individuals practice espiritismo, a blend of Native Indian, African, and Catholic beliefs that deal with rituals related to spiritual communications with spirits and evil forces. Espiritistas (individuals who communicate with spirits) may be consulted to promote spiritual wellness and treat mental illnesses. Espiritistas are often consulted to determine folk remedies compatible with Western medical treatments.
Because older persons may consider illness a result of sins, a cure should be sought through prayer or by the “laying on of hands.”

para.emphasis: Inquire about the family’s wishes regarding the Sacrament of the Sick. Clergy are a resource for spiritual wisdom and help with various spiritual needs.

listitem.para: Although amulets have lost their popularity, some still use them. An azabache (tiny black fist) or a rabbit’s foot might be used for good luck, to drive away evil spirits, and to protect a child’s health. Rosary beads and patron saint figures may be placed at the head or side of the bed or on the patient to protect them from outside evil sources. Ask permission before removing, cleaning, or moving amulets. A benediction may be requested before removing amulets or religious objects, giving the Sacrament of the Sick, or providing spiritual support.

para.emphasis: Integrate individual, family, and community resources to encourage a focus on health promotion and enhance early health screening and disease prevention. Offering weekend, evening, and late-night healthcare services increases the use of preventive services.

listitem.para: Most Puerto Ricans believe in “family care” rather than self-care. Women are considered the primary caregivers and promoters of family health and are the source of spiritual and physical strength. The involvement of the family in the provision of care for individuals who are sick is a crucial aspect to consider. Given the cultural values held by Puerto Ricans, which prioritize the significance of family, faith, and religious practices, healthcare providers should exercise care when contemplating the possibility of referring families to social support services. Acknowledge the importance of family in healthcare-related matters. Involve family members in discussions and decision-making processes.
Natural herbs, teas, and over-the-counter medications are often used as initial interventions for symptoms of illness.

Patients who use folk practices visit botánicas (folk religious stores) and use natural herbs, aromatic incense, special bathing herbs, prayer books, prayers, and figurines for treating illness and promoting good health. Refrain from making prejudicial comments that may inhibit collaboration with folk healers.
Most families prefer to keep chronically or terminally ill family members at home.

Some place a damp cloth on the forehead to refresh the “hot” inside the body and relieve nausea. Some put the head between the legs to stop vomiting. Mint, orange, or lemon-tree leaves are boiled and used as tea to relieve nausea and vomiting.

Barriers to using healthcare services include poor English language skills, low socioeconomic status, lack of insurance, and lack of transportation. Many individuals experience apprehension when requesting time off from their employment concern for childcare.

para.emphasis: Provide interpreters and offer social work or transportation services if needed.

listitem.para: Puerto Rican males may experience stigma and unease when engaging in open discussions about sexual health, primarily due to cultural norms and societal expectations. Consequently, individuals may exhibit more remarkable reticence and less inclination to pursue knowledge and assistance about matters of sexual health. Foster an environment that is inclusive and free from bias. Certain healthcare providers specialize in sexual health that can result in a higher level of expertise and sensitivity while engaging in discussions about sex-related matters.

Most Puerto Ricans tend to be loud and outspoken in expressing pain. Some prefer oral or intravenous medications for pain relief rather than intramuscular injections or rectal medications. Herbal teas, heat, and prayer are often used to manage pain.

para.emphasis: Do not censure the expression of pain or judge it as an exaggeration. This expressive behavior is a socially learned mechanism to cope with pain.
is a common verbal moaning expression for

listitem.para: Rural elderly individuals might have difficulty interpreting and quantifying pain. The use of numerical pain-identifying scales may be inappropriate.

Because mental illness carries a stigma, obtaining information or talking about mental illness may be difficult. Some families might not disclose the presence or history of mental illnesses, even in a trusting environment. They might use phrases like “ estar bajoneado/a” (feeling down) or “ tener los nervios mal o de punta” (having bad nerves) as indirect ways to refer to these conditions. However, it’s important to note that this changes as mental health awareness grows and more people openly discuss these issues. Monitor the psychological well-being considering family dynamics and refer patients to support services as appropriate.

Symptoms of mental illness are often perceived as the result of nervios (nerves), having done something wrong, or failing God’s commandments. When someone is anxious or overcome with emotions or problems, they are just nervioso. Similarly, someone experiencing a mental health concern such as melancholy, anxiety, or lack of sleep may be nervioso(a) or suffering from an ataque de nervios (nervous attack) rather than clinically depressed or mentally ill. Demonstrate empathy and respect toward the individual’s beliefs. Recognize and validate the cultural perspective of the individual while acknowledging and affirming their emotional state and worries. Communicate by adapting to the cultural understanding of the listener. Refrain from using technical terminology commonly used in the medical field. Provide psychoeducational information regarding mental health disorders, emphasizing the multifactorial etiology of these problems, such as biological, psychological, and sociocultural elements.
Acknowledge confidentiality when obtaining healthcare history. If trust is built, health-care providers may get a more accurate response to their questions.
Promote physical and mental health by encouraging the active involvement of community-based churches, schools, or childcare centers.

Organ donation is considered an act of goodwill and a gift of life. Autopsy may be regarded as a violation of the body. When discussions regarding autopsies and organ donations are necessary, proceed patiently and provide precise and straightforward information. A priest or minister is often helpful and may be expected to be present at the time of death.
Many individuals are reluctant to receive or donate blood for fear of contracting HIV.

para.emphasis: Explore beliefs about blood donation and transfusions to dispel myths.

listitem.para: Puerto Ricans hold healthcare providers in high regard. Distrust may develop if the healthcare provider (a) lacks respect for issues related to traditional health practices, (b) ignores personalism in the relationship, (c) does not use advanced technological assessment tools, and (d) has a physical or personal image that differs from the traditional “well-groomed white attire” image.

Puerto Ricans may use traditional and folk healers such as espiritistas and santeros with Western healthcare providers. Some espiritismo practices are used to deal with the power of good and evil spirits in the physical and emotional development of the individual. Santeros , individuals prepared to practice santería, are consulted in matters related to the belief of object intrusion, diseases caused by evil spirits, the loss of the soul, the insertion of a spirit, or the anger of God.

Traditional therapeutic practices like curanderismo, which mix herbal medicine, spiritual healing, and energy therapy, are just one example. Natural treatments, such as herbs and plants, are part of the healing practices employed by curanderos and curanderas. Another way that people deal with the disease is through the spiritual cleansing practice. Many Puerto Ricans put faith in the healing powers of spiritual purification. This may include using holy water or smudging negative energy away with herbs like sage or palo santo. Encourage patients to disclose any herbal remedies they are using alongside conventional treatments. Monitor the use of herbal remedies. Some herbs or vitamins may interact with prescribed medications or cause side effects: healthcare providers monitoring their use is essential for patient safety.
Men prefer male physicians and may feel embarrassed and uncomfortable with a female physician.
Sometimes, individuals may discount female physicians’ academic and intellectual competencies and distrust their judgment and treatment.
Some women feel uncomfortable with a male physician; a few prefer a male doctor.
Older people may prefer more aged healthcare providers because they are considered wise and mature in health, life experiences, and folk practices.

Some people experience adverse societal perceptions when seeking help for mental health issues. Create a clinical environment that promotes support and inclusivity, acknowledging and valuing Puerto Ricans’ unique characteristics and cultural diversity. Use a systematic approach for facilitating appropriate referrals within mental healthcare. Integrate comprehensive health education initiatives that promote the normalization of mental healthcare and well-being, emphasizing the importance of seeking help when experiencing negative emotions.
